title_en: Salah in the Shafi'i School: Conditions, Pillars, and Sunnah Acts
content_en:
The chapter on salah in Al-Iqna follows Abu Shuja's text through the conditions, pillars, invalidators, and recommended acts of prayer in the Shafi'i school. The Shafi'i tradition on prayer is notable for several positions that distinguish it from the other schools, and ash-Shirbini's commentary brings these out with careful attention to both the legal formulations and their evidential basis.

The conditions (shurut) of prayer in the Shafi'i school include: Islam, mental soundness, distinguishing ability (tamyiz — meaning the child who can understand instructions is obligated to pray in the training sense), knowledge that the prayer time has entered, ritual purity (taharah from both hadath and khabath), covering the awrah, facing the qiblah, and standing if able. The intention (niyyah) is a condition but also simultaneously the first pillar — the Shafi'i school is precise about this distinction.

The pillars (arkan) of prayer in the Shafi'i school are seventeen, a larger number than the Hanafi school's listing, reflecting the Shafi'i tendency to classify more elements as obligatory. They include: the opening takbir, standing in obligatory prayer, reciting al-Fatiha in every rak'ah (the Shafi'i position — unlike the Hanafi school which requires Fatiha only in the first two rak'ahs of a four-rak'ah prayer), bowing (ruku') with stillness (tuma'ninah), rising from ruku' with stillness, prostrating twice with stillness, sitting between the two prostrations, the final sitting for the tashahhud, the tashahhud itself, the salawat upon the Prophet in the final sitting, and the concluding salam. Tuma'ninah (stillness/tranquility in each posture) is a fard element in the Shafi'i school — a distinction from the Hanafi school where it is considered wajib but below fard.

On recitation, the Shafi'i school requires al-Fatiha in every rak'ah of every prayer, including the third and fourth rak'ahs of Dhuhr, Asr, and Isha. If a person forgets al-Fatiha in any rak'ah and remembers before ruku', they must recite it; if they have already entered ruku', that rak'ah is invalid and must be added. Ash-Shirbini explains this based on the hadith 'There is no prayer for one who does not recite the Fatiha' (al-Bukhari, Muslim) taken in its full generality.

The Shafi'i school holds that Ameen is said aloud (jahran) in loud prayers by both imam and followers. This ruling, based on hadiths recorded by Abu Dawud and Tirmidhi describing the Prophet and companions saying Ameen aloud, contrasts with the Hanafi position of silent Ameen. Ash-Shirbini notes this is an area of known difference between the schools, all of whose positions have evidential support.

The recommended acts (sunan) of prayer are extensive in the Shafi'i school. They include: raising the hands at the opening takbir, at the beginning of ruku', when rising from ruku', and when standing for the third rak'ah — based on the hadith of Ibn Umar reported in al-Bukhari; placing the right hand over the left on the chest; reciting the opening supplication; saying Ameen; reciting an additional surah after al-Fatiha; making the back level in ruku'; placing the hands on the thighs in the sitting position with the right hand making the number fifty-three (the index finger pointing, others closed); and the supplication of qunoot in Fajr prayer — a specifically Shafi'i sunnah based on authenticated hadiths of qunoot in Fajr.

Ash-Shirbini's commentary explains each sunnah with its hadith basis, distinguishing clearly between what is obligatory (fard), recommended (sunnah), and merely desirable (mustahabb). This threefold classification, applied consistently throughout the prayer chapter, gives students a clear sense of what is essential to the prayer's validity and what represents excellence and completeness of worship.
title_id: Shalat dalam Mazhab Syafi'i: Syarat, Rukun, dan Amalan Sunnah
content_id:
Bab shalat dalam Al-Iqna' mengikuti teks Abu Syuja' melalui syarat-syarat, rukun-rukun, hal-hal yang membatalkan, dan perbuatan-perbuatan yang dianjurkan dalam shalat menurut mazhab Syafi'i. Tradisi Syafi'i dalam shalat memiliki beberapa posisi yang membedakannya dari mazhab-mazhab lain, dan syarh asy-Syirbini menguraikannya dengan perhatian seksama pada rumusan hukum maupun dasar dalilnya.

Syarat-syarat (syuruth) shalat dalam mazhab Syafi'i meliputi: Islam, kesehatan akal, kemampuan membedakan (tamyiz — artinya anak yang dapat memahami instruksi diwajibkan shalat dalam arti latihan), mengetahui bahwa waktu shalat telah masuk, kesucian ritual (thaharah dari hadats dan khabats), menutup aurat, menghadap kiblat, dan berdiri jika mampu. Niat adalah syarat sekaligus rukun pertama — mazhab Syafi'i cermat dalam perbedaan ini.

Rukun-rukun (arkan) shalat dalam mazhab Syafi'i berjumlah tujuh belas, jumlah yang lebih banyak dari daftar mazhab Hanafi, mencerminkan kecenderungan Syafi'i untuk mengklasifikasikan lebih banyak unsur sebagai wajib. Rukun-rukun tersebut meliputi: takbiratul ihram, berdiri dalam shalat wajib, membaca al-Fatihah di setiap rakaat (posisi Syafi'i — berbeda dari mazhab Hanafi yang hanya mewajibkan Fatihah pada dua rakaat pertama shalat empat rakaat), ruku' dengan thuma'ninah, bangkit dari ruku' dengan thuma'ninah, sujud dua kali dengan thuma'ninah, duduk di antara dua sujud, duduk akhir untuk tasyahhud, tasyahhud itu sendiri, shalawat kepada Nabi pada duduk akhir, dan salam penutup. Thuma'ninah (ketenangan dalam setiap posisi) adalah unsur fardhu dalam mazhab Syafi'i — perbedaan dari mazhab Hanafi di mana hal itu dianggap wajib tetapi di bawah fardhu.

Dalam hal bacaan, mazhab Syafi'i mewajibkan al-Fatihah di setiap rakaat dari setiap shalat, termasuk rakaat ketiga dan keempat Dhuhur, Ashar, dan Isya. Jika seseorang lupa al-Fatihah pada suatu rakaat dan teringat sebelum ruku', ia harus membacanya; jika sudah masuk ruku', rakaat itu batal dan harus ditambahkan. Asy-Syirbini menjelaskan ini berdasarkan hadis 'Tidak ada shalat bagi orang yang tidak membaca Fatihah' (Bukhari, Muslim) yang dipahami secara umum.

Mazhab Syafi'i berpendapat bahwa Amin diucapkan dengan keras (jahran) dalam shalat keras oleh imam maupun makmum. Hukum ini, berdasarkan hadis-hadis yang diriwayatkan oleh Abu Dawud dan Tirmidzi yang menggambarkan Nabi dan para sahabat mengucapkan Amin dengan keras, berbeda dengan posisi Hanafi yang mengucapkan Amin secara pelan. Asy-Syirbini mencatat bahwa ini adalah area perbedaan yang dikenal antar mazhab, yang semua posisinya memiliki dukungan dalil.

Amalan-amalan sunnah (sunan) shalat sangat banyak dalam mazhab Syafi'i. Termasuk di antaranya: mengangkat tangan pada takbiratul ihram, saat memulai ruku', saat bangkit dari ruku', dan saat berdiri untuk rakaat ketiga — berdasarkan hadis Ibn Umar yang diriwayatkan dalam Bukhari; meletakkan tangan kanan di atas tangan kiri di dada; membaca doa iftitah; mengucapkan Amin; membaca surah tambahan setelah al-Fatihah; meratakan punggung saat ruku'; meletakkan tangan di atas paha dalam posisi duduk dengan tangan kanan membentuk angka lima puluh tiga (jari telunjuk menunjuk, jari lainnya tertutup); dan doa qunut dalam shalat Subuh — sunnah khusus Syafi'i berdasarkan hadis-hadis sahih tentang qunut Subuh.

Syarh asy-Syirbini menjelaskan setiap sunnah dengan dasar hadisnya, membedakan dengan jelas antara yang wajib (fardhu), yang dianjurkan (sunnah), dan yang sekadar disukai (mustahabb). Klasifikasi tiga tingkat ini, diterapkan secara konsisten di seluruh bab shalat, memberi para pelajar gambaran jelas tentang apa yang esensial bagi keabsahan shalat dan apa yang mewakili kesempurnaan dan kelengkapan ibadah.
content_ar: The الباب on الصلاة in Al-Iqna follows Abu Shuja's text through the conditions, pillars, invalidators, and recommended acts of الصلاة in the الشافعي school. The الشافعي التراث on الصلاة is notable for several positions that distinguish it from the other schools, and ash-Shirbini's commentary brings these out with careful attention to both the legal formulations and their evidential basis. The conditions (shurut) of الصلاة in the الشافعي school include: الإسلام, mental soundness, distinguishing ability (tamyiz — meaning the الولد who can understand instructions is obligated to pray in the training sense), العلم that the الصلاة time has entered, ritual purity (الطهارة from both hadath and khabath), covering the العورة, facing the القبلة, and standing إذا able. The intention (niyyah) is a condition but أيضاً simultaneously the أولاً pillar — the الشافعي school is precise about this distinction. The pillars (arkan) of الصلاة in the الشافعي school are seventeen, a larger number than the الحنفي school's listing, reflecting the الشافعي tendency to classify more elements as obligatory. They include: the opening التكبير, standing in obligatory الصلاة, reciting al-Fatiha in every rak'ah (the الشافعي position — unlike the الحنفي school which requires Fatiha only in the أولاً two rak'ahs of a four-rak'ah الصلاة), bowing (الركوع') with stillness (tuma'ninah), rising from الركوع' with stillness, prostrating twice with stillness, sitting between the two prostrations, the final sitting for the التشهد, the التشهد itself, the salawat upon النبي ﷺ in the final sitting, and the concluding salam. Tuma'ninah (stillness/السكينة in each posture) is a الفرض element in the الشافعي school — a distinction from the الحنفي school where it is considered الواجب but below الفرض. On التلاوة, the الشافعي school requires al-Fatiha in every rak'ah of every الصلاة, بما في ذلك the ثالثاً and رابعاً rak'ahs of Dhuhr, Asr, and Isha. إذا a person forgets al-Fatiha in any rak'ah and remembers قبل الركوع', they must recite it; إذا they have already entered الركوع', that rak'ah is invalid and must be added. Ash-Shirbini explains this based on the الحديث 'There is no الصلاة for one who does not recite the Fatiha' (البخاري, مسلم) taken in its full generality. The الشافعي school holds that آمين is said aloud (jahran) in loud الصلوات by both الإمام and followers. This ruling, based on hadiths recorded by أبو داود and الترمذي describing النبي ﷺ and الصحابة saying آمين aloud, contrasts with the الحنفي position of silent آمين. Ash-Shirbini notes this is an area of known difference between the schools, all of whose positions have evidential support. The recommended acts (sunan) of الصلاة are extensive in the الشافعي school. They include: raising the hands at the opening التكبير, at the beginning of الركوع', عندما rising from الركوع', and عندما standing for the ثالثاً rak'ah — based on the الحديث of Ibn عمر reported in البخاري; placing the righ
